speaker: Boris Cherny
text: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 30 pull requests.
summary: Boris has fully transitioned to AI-written code, shipping 10-30 PRs daily without manual editing.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Productivity per engineer has increased 200%. I have never enjoyed coding as much as I do today, because I don't have to deal with all the minutia.
summary: 200% productivity increase from AI coding tools, with higher job satisfaction due to reduced tedious work.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: In a year or two, it's not going to matter. Coding is virtually solved. I imagine a world where everyone is able to program, anyone can just build software any time.
summary: Predicts coding will be virtually solved in 1-2 years, enabling universal software creation.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Claude is starting to come up with ideas. It's looking for feedback, it's looking at bug reports, it's looking at telemetry for bug fixes, and things to ship. A little more like a coworker.
summary: AI coding agents are evolving from tools to autonomous coworkers that identify and ship improvements independently.

speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
text: I don't have a coding background. My job is to build both internal tools and customer-facing products purely using AI. The key is knowing what to build, not how to build it.
summary: Professional vibe coder with no coding background builds production tools for Lovable using only AI.

speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
text: The biggest mistake people make is starting too big. Start with something you actually need. My first project was a simple dashboard for tracking my own metrics.
summary: Start vibe coding with personal needs, not ambitious projects — the learning compounds from real usage.

speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
text: Internal tools are the sweet spot for vibe coding. The bar for quality is lower, the feedback loop is faster, and the value is immediate.
summary: Internal tools are the ideal use case for vibe coding due to lower quality bar and faster feedback loops.

speaker: Albert Cheng
text: At Duolingo, the leaderboard was transformative. But the insight wasn't 'add a leaderboard' — it was understanding that social comparison is the most powerful retention mechanic we had.
summary: Duolingo's leaderboard success was about social comparison psychology, not the mechanic itself.

speaker: Albert Cheng
text: The streak is the single most powerful feature Duolingo ever built. But it only works because we invested in making it forgiving — streak freezes, streak repair, grace periods.
summary: Duolingo's streak feature is their most powerful tool, made effective by forgiving mechanics like streak freezes.

speaker: Albert Cheng
text: Don't just look at your retention curves. Look at what your most retained users do differently in their first week. That behavioral signature is your growth playbook.
summary: Study the behavioral differences of highly retained users in their first week to find growth levers.

speaker: Elena Verna
text: The best growth teams I've seen all have one thing in common: they measure retention before acquisition. If your bucket is leaky, pouring more water in doesn't help.
summary: Top growth teams prioritize retention measurement over acquisition — fix the leaky bucket first.

speaker: Elena Verna
text: Gamification gets a bad rap because people think of it as badges and points. But the good kind — leaderboards, streaks, social proof — those are fundamentally about making progress visible.
summary: Good gamification makes progress visible through mechanics like leaderboards and streaks, not superficial badges.

speaker: Elena Verna
text: Notifications are the most underinvested growth lever. Most companies treat them as marketing spam. The companies that win treat every notification as a product decision.
summary: Notifications should be product decisions, not marketing — most companies underinvest in notification quality.

speaker: Grant Lee
text: We grew to $100M ARR largely through product-led growth. The key was making the creation experience so fast that people would share their Gamma decks, and viewers would sign up to make their own.
summary: Gamma reached $100M ARR through product-led growth driven by a fast creation experience and viral sharing.

speaker: Grant Lee
text: We use gamification elements — streak-like engagement mechanics, progress indicators — but we never call them that internally. We call them 'momentum features.'
summary: Gamma uses gamification-style mechanics internally called 'momentum features' to drive engagement.

speaker: Tomer Cohen
text: We're calling it the 'full-stack builder.' A PM who can prototype, run evals, analyze data, and ship small features independently. This is the new bar at LinkedIn.
summary: LinkedIn's new PM standard: 'full-stack builders' who can prototype, eval, analyze, and ship independently.

speaker: Tomer Cohen
text: The PMs who are thriving right now are the ones who see AI as expanding their capabilities, not threatening their role. They're building things they never could before.
summary: PMs who embrace AI as capability expansion (not threat) are thriving by building things previously impossible.

speaker: Ravi Mehta
text: I start every AI prototype with a JSON schema. Not a wireframe, not a design. JSON. Because it forces you to think about what the product actually does before how it looks.
summary: Start AI prototypes with JSON schemas to force clarity about product function before form.

speaker: Ravi Mehta
text: The best PMs I know right now are all building. Not writing specs and throwing them over the wall. Actually building prototypes, testing them with users, iterating in real-time.
summary: Top PMs are now builders — prototyping and iterating directly rather than just writing specs.

speaker: Hamel Husain
text: Evals are the new unit tests for AI. If you don't have evals, you're shipping blind. Every AI product team needs to treat evals as a first-class concern.
summary: AI evals are the equivalent of unit tests — shipping without them means shipping blind.

speaker: Shreya Shankar
text: The biggest mistake teams make is trying to automate all evals. You need human judgment in the loop, especially for anything subjective. The best systems combine both.
summary: Best eval systems combine automated metrics with human judgment — full automation fails for subjective quality.

speaker: Hamel Husain
text: PMs should own evals, not engineers. It's a product quality question, not a technical one. The PM should define what 'good' looks like.
summary: PMs, not engineers, should own AI evals because defining 'good' is a product question.

speaker: Chip Huyen
text: Product managers don't need to understand backpropagation. But they need to understand what a model can and can't do, what evals mean, and how to think about AI reliability.
summary: PMs need AI literacy at the decision-making level — not technical depth, but capability and reliability understanding.

speaker: Chip Huyen
text: Evals are the most important thing in AI engineering right now. More important than model selection, more important than prompt engineering. Get your evals right and everything else follows.
summary: Evals are the most critical AI engineering practice — more important than model or prompt selection.

speaker: Dan Shipper
text: 100% of our code is AI-written. Five products, seven-figure revenue. The quality bar has crossed the threshold where AI code is production-ready for most use cases.
summary: Every (Dan's company) runs 5 products on 100% AI-written code with 7-figure revenue, proving production viability.

speaker: Dan Shipper
text: The gap between demo and production is real. AI can write code fast, but making it reliable requires iteration. We spend about 30% of our time on polish and reliability.
summary: Demo-to-production gap is real — 30% of time goes to polish and reliability even with AI-written code.

speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
text: I'm a PM at Meta with zero coding background. I built a full internal tool using Cursor in three days. It's now used by my entire team daily.
summary: Non-technical Meta PM built a team-wide internal tool in 3 days using Cursor with no coding background.

speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
text: Start with your own pain point. Don't try to build a startup. Build something you need. The motivation to finish is completely different when you're the user.
summary: Start AI building with personal pain points — self-use motivation is the key to finishing projects.

speaker: Nicole Forsgren
text: The biggest mistake in measuring AI productivity is looking at lines of code or PRs per day. You need to measure outcomes: cycle time, change failure rate, deployment frequency.
summary: Measure AI productivity by outcomes (cycle time, failure rate, deployment frequency), not output volume.

speaker: Nicole Forsgren
text: DORA metrics still work in the AI era, but you need to add new dimensions. We're adding 'AI augmentation rate' — what percentage of work is AI-assisted vs. purely human.
summary: DORA metrics remain valid but need AI-era additions like 'AI augmentation rate' for assisted vs. human work.

speaker: Alexander Embiricos
text: Context engineering is the new prompt engineering. It's not about the single prompt — it's about what information you give the AI over the entire session.
summary: Context engineering (session-level information management) is replacing prompt engineering as the key AI skill.

speaker: Alexander Embiricos
text: The trick is parallelization. Run five agents at once on different parts of the problem. Your job becomes orchestration, not execution.
summary: Parallelizing AI agents transforms the developer role from executor to orchestrator.

speaker: Jason Cohen
text: When growth stalls, 90% of the time it's a retention problem, not an acquisition problem. But teams always want to fix acquisition first because it's more fun.
summary: Stalled growth is almost always a retention issue, but teams default to fixing acquisition because it's more engaging.

speaker: Jason Cohen
text: Logo retention is the most honest metric in B2B. Net revenue retention can mask churn with expansion. Logo retention tells you if customers are actually staying.
summary: Logo retention is more honest than NRR for B2B — it reveals real customer retention without expansion masking churn.

speaker: Matt LeMay
text: The PM role is evolving faster than any other role in tech. The PMs who survive are the ones who keep redefining their own job description every six months.
summary: PM role evolves faster than any tech role — survival requires redefining your job description every 6 months.

speaker: Matt LeMay
text: The best question a PM can ask is 'What would have to be true for this to work?' It saves you from building the wrong thing and from political landmines.
summary: The most powerful PM question: 'What would have to be true for this to work?' — prevents wrong builds and political issues.

speaker: Molly Graham
text: In high-growth, you have to hire for the role people will grow into, not the role they're perfect for today. The job changes every 6-12 months.
summary: High-growth hiring: recruit for growth potential, not current-role fit — the job changes every 6-12 months.

speaker: Marc Andreessen
text: Every PM in the world should be learning to build with AI tools. Not because they'll replace engineers, but because the ability to prototype changes the entire product development cycle.
summary: All PMs should learn AI building tools — not to replace engineers, but to fundamentally change the product development cycle.

speaker: Marc Andreessen
text: The companies that are going to win are the ones where AI isn't a feature — it's the operating system. It changes how you build, how you sell, how you support.
summary: Winning companies will use AI as their operating system — transforming building, selling, and support — not just adding it as a feature.

speaker: Bret Taylor
text: I think the PM of the future looks more like a product engineer. Someone who can think strategically but also build and test ideas rapidly. The line between PM and engineer is blurring.
summary: Future PMs will be product engineers — strategic thinkers who also build and test rapidly as the PM/engineer boundary blurs.

speaker: Brendan Foody
text: Expert evals are the competitive moat for AI companies. The companies that have the best domain experts writing evals are the ones shipping the best AI products.
summary: Expert-written evals are a competitive moat — the best AI products come from companies with the best domain expert evaluators.

speaker: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti
text: If you make a bunch of practitioners sit together and ask them, 'Is it important to build an actionable feedback loop for AI products?' All of them will agree. But almost nobody does it well.
summary: Everyone agrees AI products need feedback loops, but almost no one implements them well in practice.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: This design process that designers have been taught, where you go off and do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, converge, diverge, converge — we sort of treated as gospel. That's basically dead.
summary: The traditional design process of diverge-converge cycles that designers treated as gospel is now dead in the age of AI.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: There are basically two types of design work now. The first is supporting implementation and execution — engineers are using their seven Claudes to create features. The second is creating the vision or direction, but now it's a three to six month vision, not a two to five year one, and it's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
summary: Design work has stratified into two modes: supporting fast engineering execution and setting short-term vision through prototypes rather than beautiful long-term decks.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: It's not just designers who are feeling like, 'Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.' I think even engineers are like, 'How do we keep up with ourselves?'
summary: The pace of AI-driven engineering change is so fast that even engineers are struggling to keep up with their own capabilities.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: The way you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens. But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to maintain the brand of your company if you commit to iterating, responding to feedback, and continuously shipping improvements.
summary: Shipping early is fine for brand trust as long as you visibly iterate and respond to user feedback — what kills trust is releasing something early and then going silent.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: I think AI will get better at taste and judgment and design. We might be holding onto that a little bit too much. At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters. Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
summary: AI will improve at taste and design judgment, but humans will still be needed for accountability and deciding what actually matters to build.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: Being an IC across this past year gave me so many hard skills I wouldn't have gained if I was just managing. The design process has changed so much that I think design managers need to move back into IC work to truly understand what is happening, so they can be better managers.
summary: Design managers should rotate back into IC work to build empathy and hard skills for the rapidly changing AI-era design process.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: You have to know the difference between a megatrend and a hype cycle. When there's a megatrend, don't fight it. AI is a megatrend, one of the most foundational movements that we have seen in human history.
summary: Leaders must distinguish megatrends from hype cycles — AI is a megatrend you should never fight.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Every management book says praise in public, criticize in private. I fundamentally disagree. Establish enough trust so you are comfortable critiquing and debating in public. In private, build that trust. In public, you don't want posturing — you want problem-solving.
summary: Reverse the conventional wisdom: critique and debate in public for problem-solving, build trust and reassurance in private.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Don't delegate the storytelling. The story is not a marketing exercise after you built the product. The story is why you build the product. If you have seven or eight layers between you and the front line, you can't play the telephone game. Always own telling the story yourself.
summary: CEOs should never delegate company storytelling — the story defines why you build, and telephone-game message loss across org layers guarantees failure.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The six things you need to build a great company, in descending order: timing, market, team, product, brand, distribution. You don't have all six, you don't win. A great team in a bad market gets dragged down — the market always wins.
summary: Great companies need six elements in priority order: timing, market, team, product, brand, and distribution — the market always trumps the team.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Permission to play means asking: is this logical for us? Do we have the route to market for mass scale distribution? If you dissipate your caloric burn across too many areas, nothing gets enough girth to drive all the way through. Be extremely selective where you expend your calories.
summary: Companies should only enter markets where they have permission to play and a distribution advantage.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Persistence beats intellect, and stamina beats intellect any day of the week, twice on Sunday. The biggest reason successful leaders win is not that they're the smartest — it's that they have enormous staying power in the game.
summary: Stamina and persistence matter more than intelligence for leadership success.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The platform that you choose and the quality of problems that you pick to solve determine a lot of the path of success for you. Harder problems attract better people, and business is a team sport.
summary: Choose hard problems to attract the best talent — easy problems won't draw the team you need to win.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I look for four things in CEOs — my LOCK algorithm. L is for lovable — can I envision following this person? O is obsession with the problem. C is chip on the shoulder. K is deeply knowledgeable about the domain. And I'd add S for student — the best ones are deep, deep students of the game.
summary: The LOCKS framework for evaluating CEOs: Lovable, Obsessed, Chip-on-shoulder, Knowledgeable, and Student of the game.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: Starting a company has never been easier. The flip side is the number of companies formed is going to mushroom. There's so much noise and competition, it's just going to be hard to stand out and really accelerate and scale. It's never been easier to start, never been harder to scale.
summary: AI makes starting companies trivially easy, but the resulting explosion of competition makes scaling harder than ever.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: If you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. Every CEO at the adults' table has gone through this and they are religious about the DRI — directly responsible individual.
summary: Shared ownership kills execution at scale — every important initiative needs a single DRI.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. Immature managers solve for TV over EV. We always put on the wall: solve for CV, then EV, then TV, then MEV. Customers first, then company, then team, then yourself.
summary: The priority hierarchy for decisions should be Customer Value, Enterprise Value, Team Value, then My Value.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I always valued optionality, but there's a massive tax in optionality when you can move this fast. Planning cycles used to be a year, now they're three months.
summary: In the AI era, optionality is expensive — planning cycles have shrunk from annual to quarterly.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: The go-to-market is going to get turned on its head. People start evaluating products in Gemini or ChatGPT. Your website is a lot less important. Sites will change to have a high quality avatar that knows everything about your products.
summary: Go-to-market will be transformed as buyers research in AI chatbots instead of websites.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: IC engineers are becoming tech leads. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents. I know many engineers on my team have 10 to 20 threads being pulled on at the same time.
summary: Software engineers are transforming from code writers into tech lead managers who orchestrate 10-20 parallel AI agent threads.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The one person billion dollar startup implies that to enable it, there might be a hundred other small startups building bespoke software. We might actually enter into a golden age of B2B SaaS.
summary: The one-person billion-dollar startup will spawn a golden age of B2B SaaS with tens of thousands of $10M micro-companies.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The Mythical Man-Month predicted software engineering would go where engineers are like surgeons. As a manager, look around corners and unblock people, especially from organizational bottlenecks.
summary: Managers should treat engineers like surgeons — proactively removing organizational blockers.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: Companies where AI really works have top-down buy-in combined with bottoms-up adoption. A lot of AI deployments fail because it's an exec mandate, extremely top-down, divorced from what actual work looks like.
summary: Successful AI adoption requires both executive buy-in and bottoms-up evangelism from an internal tiger team.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. Build for where the models are going, not where they are today.
summary: AI models keep consuming the scaffolding built around them, so builders should target where model capabilities are heading.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: We live in a bubble in Silicon Valley. A lot of work that runs our entire economy is not open-ended knowledge work — it's repeatable business processes with standard operating procedures.
summary: Business process automation is a massively underrated AI opportunity.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: Every startup that I've seen fizzle out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come and squashed them. It's because they built something that didn't resonate with customers.
summary: Startups fail from lack of customer resonance, not from being crushed by OpenAI.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: Repair is the number one relationship strategy we have. Secure attachment isn't defined by getting it right all the time — it's defined by an adult who's willing to repair.
summary: Repair — taking responsibility after you mess up — is the most powerful relationship strategy.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: The idea of being good inside inherently requires us to separate behavior and identity. When someone's late to work a lot, start with: 'This is a good person who is late.'
summary: Separate behavior from identity to have productive conversations.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: Picture a turbulent flight. The sturdy pilot says: 'I hear you screaming. It makes sense. I know what I'm doing. This turbulence doesn't scare me. I'll see you when we land.' That's sturdy leadership.
summary: Sturdy leadership means acknowledging others' emotions as real without being overwhelmed by them.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: Boundaries are what you tell someone else you will do, and it requires the other person to do nothing. Too often people confuse requests with boundaries.
summary: True boundaries define what you will do and require nothing from the other person.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: Optimizing for happiness in childhood is the quickest way to build anxiety and fragility in adulthood. When we're thinking about a resilient work culture, we want people who can say, 'This is hard and I can do hard things.'
summary: Optimizing for happiness over resilience creates fragility.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: Dealing with anxiety is a combination of 'I believe you and I believe in you.' Picture someone in a hole — they need you to have one foot in the hole with them and one foot outside.
summary: Effective leadership combines validation with confidence in someone's capability.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
text: The most generous interpretation — MGI. The story you tell yourself about your organization at night becomes the leader you are the next morning.
summary: The Most Generous Interpretation (MGI) transforms how leaders respond to difficult behavior.

speaker: Sam Lessin
text: There has to be a deeper truth to it. There's a serious real narrative to why etiquette matters in 2025 for founders.
summary: Etiquette is a critical business skill for founders because building trust matters more than ever.

speaker: Sam Lessin
text: Etiquette is a skill for how to show up in a room with a low heart rate. You kind of want to show up with the self-confidence and the calm of abundance.
summary: Etiquette is fundamentally about showing up with an abundance mindset and low heart rate.

speaker: Sam Lessin
text: Coming in and saying 'Hey let me give you my four-minute startup spiel' is so self-centered. Think of it like ping pong — hit the ball back.
summary: The best networking conversations are balanced exchanges like ping pong, not one-sided monologues.

speaker: Sam Lessin
text: Vibe coding is exactly good for this — Cursor and DigitalOcean and Cloudflare will get you a long way. But vibe coding also doesn't really scale.
summary: Vibe coding tools are great for building personal projects and MVPs but don't scale.

speaker: Sam Lessin
text: Seed VCs who invest in companies branded as AI companies are going to lose an impossibly large amount of money. There's a difference between a great business using AI versus an 'AI company.'
summary: AI-branded startups are terrible seed investments because they are too capital intensive and commoditizing.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: However you get those first 10, 15, 20 customers, be honest. If they need a sales type motion and you say 'I don't like sales, so I'm going to do a PLG motion,' you'll fail.
summary: Founders must be honest about whether their customers need a sales-led motion rather than forcing PLG.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: Almost all founders are A+ middlers. Any founder should be there by day one. And prospects love it — they get to talk to you. It's magical for early adopters.
summary: Founders are naturally great at the middle part of sales conversations because of their deep product knowledge.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: Those first couple reps have to be people you would buy your own product from. We're looking for pirates and romantics in the early days.
summary: Your first sales hires must be people you would personally buy your own product from.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: You need two sales reps hitting quota before you're ready to hire a manager for them. If you hire a VP of sales before then, it's approaching 100% chance of failure.
summary: Hiring a VP of Sales before you have two reps hitting quota is almost guaranteed to fail.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: What do you want to do your first 30 days? In B2B, if I don't hear 'I'm going to go meet customers,' I'm out.
summary: The ultimate screening question for any VP hire is whether they want to meet customers in their first two weeks.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: Trust me, hire someone whose last product was harder to sell. If you hire someone from something easier, they will have none of the skills.
summary: Always hire salespeople whose previous product was harder to sell than yours.

speaker: Jason Lemkin
text: In sales, there's rules of eight. Eight SDRs need one manager, eight AEs need a director above them. You can build your whole org with eights.
summary: Scale your sales org using the rule of eight and concentrate leads on fewer, better closers.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: It is really important to me that we feel that we've deliberately understaffed every project at the company.
summary: Deliberate understaffing prevents teams from working on low-priority items and forces focus.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: You don't really learn from your mistakes, you learn from your successes. As an early career PM, you should join a winning team.
summary: You learn far more from being part of a winning team than from failure.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: Processes exist for the sole purpose of lowering beta — decreasing volatility. The downside is they suppress alpha.
summary: Use the alpha/beta framework: processes lower volatility but suppress outperformance.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: Product market fit is a sort of thing where you absolutely know it when you see it. The Silicon Valley 'never quit' mindset is not pro-entrepreneur, it's pro-venture capitalist.
summary: If you don't obviously have product-market fit, you don't have it — and 'never quit' advice serves VCs, not founders.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The market is immutable. No amount of tweeting, advertising is going to change whether the market wants your product.
summary: Product-market fit is like drug receptor binding — the market wants your product or it does not.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: Point solutions don't have enough data in the age of AI to be useful. If you own the mine with the data, you can make money.
summary: In the AI era, point SaaS solutions lack sufficient data context — only companies owning first-party data capture value.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The purest form of ambition and most intense source of energy in the business is the founder CEO. Every next concentric circle of management has the potential to be an order of magnitude drop off in intensity.
summary: Executive leadership intensity drops exponentially with each management layer.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: We basically never wanted to play the Silicon Valley game. We could fire 90% of the people and we would move faster.
summary: Surge rejected the Silicon Valley playbook and built to a billion in revenue with under 100 people.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: Imagine you wanted to train a model to write an eight line poem about the moon. Most people check: Is this a poem? Does it contain eight lines? But we are looking for Nobel Prize-winning poetry.
summary: The difference between mediocre and exceptional AI training data is the difference between checking boxes and demanding Nobel Prize-level quality.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: Instead of building AI that will actually advance us as a species, we are optimizing for AI slop instead. We're basically teaching our models to chase dopamine instead of truth.
summary: AI labs are optimizing for engagement and flashy leaderboard metrics rather than truth and accuracy.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: An RL environment is essentially a simulation of the real world. We might build a world where you have a startup with Gmail, Slack, Jira, GitHub. And then suddenly AWS goes down. Model, what do you do?
summary: RL environments simulating messy real-world scenarios are the next frontier of AI training.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: Do you want a model that says 'There are definitely 20 more ways to improve this email' or one that's optimizing for your time and just says 'Your email's great, just send it'?
summary: AI models will differentiate based on whether they optimize for engagement or for user productivity.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: The standard playbook is to get product market fit by pivoting every two weeks, chase growth with dark patterns, blitz scale. I've always disagreed. Just build the one thing only you could build.
summary: Reject the Silicon Valley playbook — build the one company that only you could build based on your unique insight.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: Vibe coding is over-hyped. People don't realize how much it's going to make your systems unmaintainable in the long-term.
summary: Vibe coding is overhyped because short-term gains mask long-term technical debt.

speaker: Jeanne DeWitt Grosser
text: With AI, it's just intensified because you have 10 players pursuing the same market opportunity and so your ability to actually bring the product to market has become more strategically important.
summary: AI has intensified competition, making GTM execution a strategic differentiator.

speaker: Jeanne DeWitt Grosser
text: 80% of customers buy to avoid pain or reduce risk as opposed to increased upside. For enterprises, you're avoiding the risk of not making your revenue target next quarter.
summary: Enterprise buyers are primarily motivated by avoiding pain and reducing risk, not by upside potential.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: Most leaders, especially technical leaders, assume they have to have all the answers. But great leaders know that you're training your team to come to you with all of the hard problems.
summary: Leaders who always provide answers inadvertently train their teams to be dependent.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: There's actually three levels to listening. Level one is internal. Level two is focused. Level three is global listening — you hear what they're communicating, not just saying.
summary: The three levels of listening framework distinguishes between self-focused, word-focused, and deep empathetic listening.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: I use a GROW model. G is goal, R is reality, O is options, W is way forward.
summary: The GROW coaching model provides a simple framework for leaders to coach rather than dictate.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: We operate in tech. We're supposed to give all of ourselves purely logically. That's not at all true. The goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding.
summary: Tech work is deeply emotional despite its logical veneer, and the goal of conflict should be mutual understanding.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: My litmus test is 80%. If you're 80% of the time in your gifts, how much energy you have to give to the world.
summary: Aim to spend 80% of your work time in your natural strengths and gifts to maximize energy and impact.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: 65% of startups fail because of co-founder conflict. Just like couples need a date night, co-founders need regular check-ins.
summary: Co-founder relationships need intentional maintenance through regular check-ins.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: Would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role? That's the question we always asked at Stripe.
summary: The 'enthusiastic rehire' test provides binary clarity for evaluating whether someone is right for their role.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: The one-page plan puts your vision and values on the first column, strategic intentions and KPIs on the second, annual goals on the third, and quarterly goals on the fourth.
summary: A one-page plan aligning vision, strategy, annual goals, and quarterly goals creates clarity across the organization.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: I feel like what we have right now is just a giant piece of shit. If you can't see almost limitless opportunities to improve, then you shouldn't be designing the product.
summary: A perpetual dissatisfaction with your product is essential for great product design.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: Along with reduced friction, 'reduce the number of clicks' is almost always exactly the wrong thing. If it takes eight clicks but every one is trivially easy, that's great.
summary: Reducing clicks is the wrong goal — instead focus on reducing cognitive load per interaction.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: At more than one company all hands, I made everyone repeat: In the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value that we create for customers.
summary: Slack's company mantra was that long-term success is measured solely by value created for customers.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: Hyper-realistic work-like activities are superficially identical to work but actually fake. Every executive will do it.
summary: Organizations fill up with 'hyper-realistic work-like activities' that look identical to real work but produce no value.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: The overwhelming majority of people you hire want to hire more people who report to them because headcount correlates with career trajectory.
summary: Parkinson's Law: nearly everyone wants to hire more reports because headcount correlates with career trajectory.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: You're not just responsible for creating the product, but also creating the market. It's almost impossible to create a new idea in someone's head.
summary: Founders must create both the product and the market, positioning through familiar concepts.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: The owner's delusion is when you think your thing is so important that you don't recognize users are regular human beings who will bounce in a fraction of a second.
summary: The 'owner's delusion' is when builders overestimate user attention and investment.

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
text: You have to be coldly rational about pivoting because it's humiliating. It feels better to keep doing it until it dies of suffocation.
summary: Pivoting requires cold rationality because emotional cost causes most founders to ride a failing idea to its death.

speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
text: Whatever AI does, currently or in the future, is up to us. Every technology is a double-edged sword.
summary: AI's impact on humanity is determined by human choices.

speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
text: We curated 15 million images on the internet, created a taxonomy of 22,000 concepts. That combination of big data, neural network, and GPU was the golden recipe for modern AI.
summary: ImageNet's combination of big data, neural networks, and GPUs created the 'golden recipe' that sparked the deep learning revolution.

speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
text: In the middle of 2015, 2016, some tech companies avoided using the word AI because they were not sure if AI was a dirty word.
summary: As recently as 2015-2016, major tech companies avoided the term 'AI' entirely.

speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
text: I realized the North Star challenge of AI is visual intelligence. Our human brains use about 50% of our neurons on visual processing. This is fundamentally a big data problem.
summary: The ImageNet breakthrough came from recognizing that AI visual intelligence was fundamentally a data problem.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: A lot of early stage founders get tripped up taking late stage sales advice. The founder is the product.
summary: Early-stage founders fail by following late-stage sales advice — the founder IS the product.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: One of the worst things someone can say is 'we're better than X product.' Better is a dangerous place. You need to be different, not better.
summary: Pitching as 'better' is a trap — position as fundamentally different and counterintuitive.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: Founder led sales is not about revenue on day one. It is about learning as fast as humanly possible.
summary: Founder-led sales has two phases: first 'sales for research' then 'sales for revenue'.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: On the first call, the demo is the only card you control. Once they see it, that dreaminess is gone. Leave them wanting more.
summary: Withhold the product demo on the first sales call to preserve mystery.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: Everyone says they have a bottom of funnel problem. It's never a bottom of funnel problem. It's always qualification.
summary: Sales problems that appear bottom-of-funnel are almost always top-of-funnel qualification issues.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: 40 to 50% of B2B SaaS startups have to sell some form of service before they can sell a technology.
summary: Nearly half of B2B SaaS startups need to sell services first to educate buyers.

speaker: Jen Abel
text: Trust is the number one currency in sales. Don't try to sell something just to prove to investors you sold something.
summary: Trust is the ultimate sales currency.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: Investors gave really helpful feedback, often in the form of rejection. I would add a new page showing how big the market was.
summary: Melanie Perkins treated each of her 100+ investor rejections as product feedback.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: The thing that I love about a crazy big goal is that you feel completely inadequate before it. You want to work really hard to will it into existence.
summary: Crazy big goals work because they create a sense of inadequacy that drives relentless effort.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: I don't have emails on my phone. When I shut my laptop, I actually tune out. Sometimes you can miss the forest from the trees when you're just working harder.
summary: Creating hard boundaries between work and rest provides the perspective to work on the right things.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: We get more than a million requests from our community every year. This year we've closed more than 200 loops.
summary: Canva processes over a million community requests annually, treating user feedback as half of their product strategy.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: Step one, build one of the world's most valuable companies. Step two, do the most good we can do. They fuel each other.
summary: Canva's two-step plan combines building a valuable company with doing maximum good, and they are mutually reinforcing.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: We didn't worry about competitors at all. It's better to solve a small number of people's problem really well.
summary: Canva succeeded by ignoring competitors and deeply solving a small group's pain point.

speaker: Melanie Perkins
text: There's many times we took bricks from someone else's house, and they didn't match. Confidence in how we take what is authentic to us and do it at the next level of scale is constant work.
summary: Importing practices from other companies often fails — authenticity at each stage of scale matters more.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: We find engineering teams that are very AI forward are reporting about eight to 10 hours saved per week. This is the worst it will ever be. This is now the baseline.
summary: AI-forward engineering teams at Block save 8-10 hours per week, and this represents the floor of AI productivity gains.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: He built this system where Goose is essentially just watching everything he does all the time. A few hours later Goose has already tried to build that feature and opened a PR.
summary: A Block engineer built a system where Goose watches his screen and Slack, autonomously building features and opening PRs.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: Vibe coding is highly limiting. We're trying to push Goose to work not just for five minutes at a time but for hours.
summary: Block is pushing beyond vibe coding toward autonomous agents that work for hours overnight.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: One of the things I do regularly is just throw away huge amounts of code. What would our world look like if every release deleted the entire app and rebuilt from scratch?
summary: AI enables a radical new workflow where engineers throw away entire systems and rebuild from scratch.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: The non-technical people using AI agents and programming tools to build things is really what's been surprising and amazing.
summary: The biggest surprise in AI adoption at Block is non-technical people using coding agents.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: A lot of engineers think code quality is important to building a successful product. The two have nothing to do with each other. YouTube was storing videos as blobs in MySQL.
summary: Code quality and product success are unrelated — YouTube beat Google Video despite terrible architecture.

speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
text: The power of Conway's Law — how difficult it is to change outcomes without changing the structure of relationships between people.
summary: Conway's Law is viscerally real at scale — you cannot change outcomes without changing org structure.

speaker: Robby Stein
text: AI is expansionary. There's actually just more and more questions being asked and curiosity that can be fulfilled now with AI.
summary: AI is not replacing traditional search but expanding it — people are asking more questions than ever.

speaker: Robby Stein
text: You need to be the physical manifestation of relentlessness. My wife described me in one word: dissatisfied. It's not unhappiness — you want to make it better.
summary: The best product builders embody relentless dissatisfaction — an inability to accept the status quo.

speaker: Robby Stein
text: For Stories, we knew we needed to do something new. We made a bunch of decisions that made it Instagram — letting people upload from camera roll, adding pause.
summary: When bringing Stories to Instagram, the key was making it complementary and distinctly Instagram.

speaker: Robby Stein
text: If I wrote a book on building great products, it would have three chapters: jobs-to-be-done, analytical rigor in root cause analysis, and designing for clarity instead of cleverness.
summary: Three principles for great products: jobs-to-be-done, root cause analysis, and clarity over cleverness.

speaker: Robby Stein
text: Close Friends totally failed originally. We found it worked for people who added 20 to 30 people because two would reply via DM.
summary: Instagram Close Friends failed until they discovered the magic threshold of 20-30 friends.

speaker: Robby Stein
text: There's this cult of lean, scrappy, fast. I see the opposite more often — people hold on to small teams too long and the product dies on the vine.
summary: The cult of lean teams can be counterproductive — under-investing kills products on the vine.

speaker: Jason Droege
text: These things take 6 to 12 months to get truly robust. Like with any major tech revolution, headlines tell one story and on the ground, you need to dig up every road.
summary: Enterprise AI takes 6-12 months to automate important processes — the gap between hype and reality is vast.

speaker: Jason Droege
text: The general trend is going from models knowing things to models doing things. That's where environments come into play.
summary: The fundamental AI shift is from models knowing things to models doing things.

speaker: Jason Droege
text: 18 months ago, you would get a short story. Now one task is building an entire website by one of the world's best developers. These tasks now take hours and require PhDs.
summary: Data labeling has evolved from simple preference ranking to multi-hour expert tasks requiring PhDs.

speaker: Jason Droege
text: If you have a human process that is 10 or 20% accurate, AI is awesome. If you have one at 98% accurate and expect the remaining 2%, not totally there yet.
summary: AI excels at improving low-accuracy processes but struggles with the last 2% on already-accurate ones.

speaker: Jason Droege
text: From an entrepreneurship standpoint, it truly is about what insight do I have? Why am I so lucky to have this insight?
summary: The fundamental entrepreneurship question is why you specifically have a unique insight.

speaker: Jason Droege
text: A document that reads the same words in company A will have a different meaning in company B. Digitizing judgment is becoming a bottleneck.
summary: The new bottleneck in enterprise AI is digitizing company-specific human judgment.

speaker: Asha Sharma
text: We're approaching a world where the marginal cost of good output approaches zero. The org chart starts to become the work chart.
summary: As marginal cost of output approaches zero, organizations will flatten from hierarchical org charts to task-based work charts.

speaker: Asha Sharma
text: These models are living organisms that get better with more interactions. This is the new IP of every company — products that think and live and learn.
summary: Products are shifting from static artifacts to living organisms that improve with every interaction.

speaker: Asha Sharma
text: We think about what season are we in? A season can be denoted by secular changes in the industry. We have loose quarterly OKRs and 4-6 week goals.
summary: Microsoft's AI Platform team plans in seasons defined by industry shifts rather than fixed timelines.

speaker: Asha Sharma
text: WhatsApp didn't win because it had stickers or stories. It won because of the phone book, reliability, and privacy.
summary: Winning products succeed on invisible infrastructure, not features.

speaker: Asha Sharma
text: I've learned that optimism is a renewable resource. Satya's ability to generate energy and renew dedication to the mission is unbelievable.
summary: Optimism is a renewable leadership resource that generates energy and renews commitment.

speaker: Asha Sharma
text: With agents and products that reason, there's a new wave around reinforcement learning. I believe we will see as much money on post-training as pre-training.
summary: Post-training investment will surpass pre-training as reinforcement learning becomes the dominant product technique.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: We're the largest expert network in the world. The only moat in human data is access to an audience.
summary: In the human data market, the only real moat is access to an audience.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: The models have gotten so good that generalists are no longer needed. We have 500,000 PhDs, 3 million master students.
summary: AI models have outgrown generalist data labelers and now need domain experts.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: Model builders care about three things: quality first, then volume, then speed.
summary: AI labs prioritize quality above all else, then volume in expert domains, then speed.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: We started to see direct outreach from the Frontier Labs trying to cut out the middleman. Five months later we were working with arguably the number one lab.
summary: Handshake went from exploration to working with the top lab in five months by going direct.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: Separate engineering team, separate design team, separate finance team. People only had one job — making Handshake AI successful.
summary: Handshake succeeded by making their AI business fully separate with dedicated teams and founder-mode intensity.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: We had this motto: leave nothing to chance. There will never be a time like this. I've never seen anything like it.
summary: In moments of unlimited demand, maximize execution intensity because these windows close fast.

speaker: Garrett Lord
text: That's not what we're hearing from employers. Being AI native, young people are at a huge advantage.
summary: Being AI-native is the new generational advantage, like growing up with Google Search.

speaker: Eoghan McCabe
text: We were about to hit $0 net new ARR, which means we would've been in negative growth territory.
summary: Intercom was on the verge of negative growth before their AI pivot.

speaker: Eoghan McCabe
text: I said, we need to become a wartime company. I rewrote the values designed to be a sharp knife to cut out the parts of the company that wouldn't be effective.
summary: McCabe declared wartime mode and rewrote company values as a tool to remove ineffective parts of the organization.

speaker: Eoghan McCabe
text: Fin is our AI agent who will pass 100 million ARR in less than three quarters.
summary: Intercom's AI agent Fin is on track to exceed $100M ARR in under three quarters.

speaker: Eoghan McCabe
text: People abhorred our pricing. It was a meme. Part of the problem was our strategy, super unfocused.
summary: Unfocused product strategy directly causes complex, hated pricing.

speaker: Eoghan McCabe
text: You don't have a choice. AI is going to disrupt in the most aggressive violent ways. If you're not in it, you're about to get kicked out.
summary: AI disruption is not optional — companies that don't embrace it will be displaced.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: This is a pattern with AI, you won't know what to polish until after you ship. My dream is that we ship daily.
summary: In AI products, you cannot know what to polish before shipping — speed beats craft early on.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: If we're shipping a feature and it doesn't get 2X better as the model gets 2X smarter, it's probably not a feature we should be shipping.
summary: Features should scale with model intelligence, otherwise they may not be worth building.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: I started writing evals before I knew what an eval was because I was just outlining clearly specified ideal behavior.
summary: Evals are simply articulating success criteria and bridge the gap between product and research teams.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: You're going to be polishing the wrong things in this space. You won't know what to polish until after you ship.
summary: Traditional product craft can be counterproductive in AI because emergent properties mean you'll polish the wrong things.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: It is a small team running ChatGPT. I take inspiration from WhatsApp. You have to treat hiring like executive recruiting.
summary: ChatGPT runs on a small team using executive-style recruiting to fill specific skill gaps.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: We would have immense regret if you had a model that was state-of-the-art on health bench and didn't use that to help people.
summary: OpenAI chose to lean into high-stakes use cases rather than disabling them.

speaker: Nick Turley
text: Curiosity is an attribute that we think matters so much more than your ML knowledge.
summary: OpenAI values curiosity over ML experience when hiring.

speaker: Chip Conley
text: I was mentoring Brian, but he was also my boss. I was 52, the average age was 26. I had to be both wise and curious.
summary: Conley describes the paradox of being a senior mentor reporting to a younger CEO.

speaker: Chip Conley
text: If what AI cannot do is the human wisdom piece, is it possible that older managers with more emotional intelligence can be a value?
summary: AI may make human wisdom and emotional intelligence — often found in older workers — more valuable.

speaker: Chip Conley
text: Show up with curiosity and a passionate engagement. People won't notice your wrinkles as much as they'll notice your energy.
summary: The key to thriving as an older person in tech is showing up with curiosity and energy.

speaker: Chip Conley
text: We are moving from the era of specialists to generalists. AI is accelerating this.
summary: AI is accelerating the shift from specialists to generalists.

speaker: Chip Conley
text: The employee model is simple: compensation at the base, recognition in the middle, meaning at the top.
summary: Conley's Maslow-inspired employee pyramid: compensation, recognition, meaning.

speaker: Chip Conley
text: Culture is what happens around here when the boss is not around. The more distributed, the more culture is important.
summary: Culture matters most in distributed companies because it guides decisions when leadership is absent.

speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
text: When we talk about pricing, many people gravitate to dollar figures. But price is a measure of value.
summary: Price should be understood as a measure of value, and willingness-to-pay conversations should happen earlier.

speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
text: They moved the price from 79 to 99, and built a decoy at 299. It was a 30+ percent increase in MRR.
summary: Using a decoy product at a higher price tier drove 30%+ MRR increase with zero product changes.

speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
text: The Panini effect — show products as a puzzle and 40 to 50% of people start taking more products because there's a compulsion to finish.
summary: The Panini effect leverages collection-completion compulsion, driving 40-50% higher attach rates.

speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
text: Don't give the farm away in your entry level product. Use the compromise effect so people avoid the extremes.
summary: Reserve features for higher tiers and use the compromise effect in packaging.

speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
text: Before you discount, think about what value can you exchange. Use non-pricing levers: give more product, change contract terms, change payment terms.
summary: Never just cut price — use non-pricing levers like giving more product or adjusting terms.

speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
text: 90% of customers who claim to have a land and expand strategy are only landing. They're not expanding because they gave their farm away.
summary: Most companies fail at land-and-expand because they give away too much value in the initial land.

speaker: Benjamin Mann
text: I think 50th percentile chance of hitting some kind of superintelligence is now like 2028.
summary: Anthropic co-founder estimates 50% chance of superintelligence by 2028.

speaker: Benjamin Mann
text: We felt like safety wasn't the top priority there. The case for safety has gotten a lot more concrete.
summary: Mann left OpenAI because safety was not the top priority.

speaker: Benjamin Mann
text: Once we get to superintelligence, it will be too late to align the models. My best forecast for an extremely bad outcome is 0-10%.
summary: AI alignment must be solved before superintelligence arrives.

speaker: Benjamin Mann
text: In a world of abundance where labor is almost free, what do jobs even look like?
summary: Post-singularity economics will look nothing like today.

speaker: Benjamin Mann
text: The talent wars are real. The expected value of a top researcher is just astronomical.
summary: Massive AI researcher compensation reflects the astronomical expected value of a single brilliant researcher.

speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
text: You don't want to walk into the gym on day one and try and deadlift 300 pounds.
summary: First-time founders should start with simpler businesses rather than trying to build the next great AI company.

speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
text: The biggest mistakes I've made have been going into business models where other people have repeatedly failed.
summary: Entrepreneurs should avoid business models with high failure rates.

speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
text: It's like having the world's most reliable employee who costs $200 a month. Do all jobs just become a single prompt?
summary: AI is the most reliable and cheapest employee — the key question is whether knowledge work reduces to a single prompt.

speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
text: Charlie Munger says, 'Fish where the fish are.' Find a small fishing hole with lots of fish and very little competition.
summary: The best business opportunities are in unsexy niches with low competition.

speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
text: In order to know what problems are valuable to solve, you kind of need to have valuable problems.
summary: Understanding how much people will actually pay to solve a problem requires real-world business experience.

speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
text: What you want is problems where the pain level is really high and the existing solutions are really bad.
summary: The best startup ideas target problems with high pain levels and poor existing solutions.

speaker: Howie Liu
text: If you were founding a new company from scratch with the same mission, how would you execute using a fully AI native approach?
summary: Every company should ask whether they would build completely differently with AI-native architecture.

speaker: Howie Liu
text: As a PM, you need to start looking more like a hybrid PM prototyper, who has some good design sensibilities.
summary: The most successful product people in the AI era are hybrid PM-prototyper-designers.

speaker: Howie Liu
text: To be continuously relevant, you have to be of the details. There is no looking at it from 10,000 foot view.
summary: CEOs must be deeply involved in product details to maintain product-market fit in the AI era.

speaker: Howie Liu
text: It's actually now hard to taste the soup without participating in creating it. To understand the solution space, you have to be in the details.
summary: Leaders must participate in building with AI to understand what is possible.

speaker: Howie Liu
text: If you want to cancel all your meetings for a day or entire week and just go play around with every AI product, go do it.
summary: Howie encourages his team to cancel meetings and spend entire days exploring AI products.

speaker: Howie Liu
text: This person has been tweeting spicy takes not substantiated by real data, yet this particular tweet went super viral.
summary: Viral misinformation showed how unverified hot takes can spread faster than corrections.

speaker: Scott Wu
text: Our whole team is only 15 engineers. Most folks work with up to five Devins at once. Devin merges several hundred pull requests into production every month.
summary: Cognition's 15-person team each runs up to 5 Devin agents, producing hundreds of PRs monthly.

speaker: Scott Wu
text: Defining the problem is probably 10% of the average software engineer's time. 90% is Kubernetes errors, debugging, migrations. Devin allows engineers to go from bricklayer to architect.
summary: AI agents let engineers shift from spending 90% on implementation to focusing on architecture.

speaker: Scott Wu
text: Absolutely yes, you should still learn to code. Most of what you're learning is the ability to logically break down problems.
summary: Learning to code remains essential because it teaches precise logical reasoning.

speaker: Scott Wu
text: Jevons Paradox says that as the price goes down, total spend can go up. As programming becomes more effective, we're going to have a lot more programmers.
summary: Jevons Paradox predicts AI will create more programmers and more software, not fewer.

speaker: Scott Wu
text: Devin is best on tasks that are well-defined. Quick front-end features, bug fixes, testing — things easy to verify.
summary: AI coding agents work best with well-defined tasks that have clear verification criteria.

speaker: Scott Wu
text: We call it a jagged intelligence. Devin can be almost like a staff engineer at understanding the code base.
summary: AI coding agents have jagged intelligence — staff-level at code comprehension but junior-level at other tasks.

speaker: Ethan Smith
text: Answer Engine Optimization is how do I show up in LLMs as an answer? You need to get mentioned as many times as possible.
summary: AEO differs from SEO because LLMs summarize multiple citations, so frequency of mentions matters most.

speaker: Ethan Smith
text: LLM leads are significantly more valuable. Webflow saw a 6X conversion rate difference between LLM traffic and Google Search traffic.
summary: LLM-referred traffic converts at 6X the rate of Google Search traffic.

speaker: Ethan Smith
text: For Google SEO, you need domain authority. For AEO, you can get mentioned by a citation tomorrow. Early-stage companies can win quickly.
summary: Unlike traditional SEO, AEO lets early-stage startups show up in LLM answers immediately.

speaker: Ethan Smith
text: Reddit is hugely cited in LLMs. Find a thread that is part of a citation. Say who you are and give useful information. Even five comments could be great.
summary: The most effective Reddit strategy for AEO is authentic, transparent participation.

speaker: Ethan Smith
text: One out of 20 landing pages drive roughly 85% of all your traffic. If you knew the few things that would work, you could push all that money to that one page.
summary: Only 5% of landing pages drive 85% of traffic, so concentrate investment in fewer, higher-quality pages.

speaker: Ethan Smith
text: ChatGPT and Google Search citation overlap was around 35%. Perplexity was around 70%.
summary: ChatGPT and Google have only 35% citation overlap.

speaker: Ben Horowitz
text: The worst thing you do as a leader is hesitate on the next decision. Both decisions are horrible. Choose quickly.
summary: Great leadership means choosing quickly between bad options rather than hesitating.

speaker: Ben Horowitz
text: The psychological muscle you have to build is to look into the abyss and go, 'that way's slightly better.' If everybody agrees, you didn't add value.
summary: A leader's value comes from making decisions that most people disagree with or are afraid to make.

speaker: Ben Horowitz
text: What I was trying to get out in Good Product Manager, Bad Product Manager was the job is fundamentally a leadership job. Nobody actually reports to you.
summary: Product management is fundamentally a leadership role made difficult by having no direct reports.

speaker: Ben Horowitz
text: All plane crashes are a series of bad decisions. None by themselves is that bad. Success works the same way.
summary: Both failure and success are the compound result of many small decisions.

speaker: Ben Horowitz
text: If you don't trust what you see and run at it, you're just not going to be good. CEOs are like great athletes.
summary: Great CEOs must trust their instincts and act immediately on what they see.

speaker: Ben Horowitz
text: A CEO said, 'My CTO is an asshole.' He didn't want to fire him. That's what you're really saying — you don't know how to have the hard conversation.
summary: The hardest leadership skill is having difficult conversations with valuable people without losing them.
